title: An Entanglement Measure from Quantum Optimal Transport
authors: Enmin Shao, Lin Chen, Huixia He
abstract: We propose a bipartite entanglement measure $E(ρ)$ defined as the minimal order-1 quantum Wasserstein distance from a state to the set of separable states. Owing to the universal data-processing inequality of the Wasserstein metric, the measure satisfies all fundamental axioms within a single geometric framework. A Lipschitz dual formulation yields explicit lower bounds for pure and mixed states, a sharp constant for two-qubit systems, and an expected value for Haar-random pure states. We further establish a quantitative connection to entanglement witnesses: any negative witness expectation value certifies a lower bound on $E$, and the dual variational bound is exactly the maximal violation achievable by a Lipschitz-1 witness. The approach naturally provides subadditivity, trace-distance estimates, and bounds on local observables, while pointing toward large-deviation conjectures. This work furnishes a versatile paradigm at the interface of entanglement theory, optimal transport, and experimental entanglement detection.
summary: This work proposes a novel entanglement measure, $E(ho)$, defined using the quantum Wasserstein distance between a state and the set of separable states. By leveraging the metric properties of the Wasserstein distance, the authors establish that $E(ho)$ satisfies all necessary axioms for an entanglement measure. This provides a versatile, geometrically grounded tool for quantifying quantum correlations.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a rigorous, axiom-satisfying measure for quantifying quantum entanglement in bipartite systems.
main_result: It introduces the entanglement measure $E(ho)$ based on the quantum Wasserstein distance, which satisfies fundamental axioms and provides quantitative lower bounds related to entanglement witnesses.
method: The core method involves defining $E(ho)$ as the minimal order-1 quantum Wasserstein distance from a state $ho$ to the set of separable states ($	ext{SEP}$), and utilizing its dual variational formulation.
model_or_system: The analysis is focused on bipartite quantum states $ho$ acting on a tensor product Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}_A \otimes \mathcal{H}_B$. The measure quantifies the distance from $ho$ to the set of separable states.
key_observables: Entanglement measure $E(ho)$, quantum Wasserstein distance $W_1(ho, \sigma)$, and entanglement witnesses $W$.
important_parameters: The Lipschitz seminorm ($\lVert H Vert_{	ext{Lip}}$) and the dimension of the subsystems ($d_A, d_B$).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on the data-processing inequality of the Wasserstein metric and the assumption that the infimum/maximum operations can be interchanged.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the measure via the Wasserstein distance, derives its dual variational form, proves it satisfies key axioms (LOCC monotonicity, etc.), and provides explicit lower bounds for pure and mixed states.
why_interesting: The geometric approach using optimal transport provides a powerful, mathematically rigorous framework for entanglement quantification, connecting it directly to measurable quantities like entanglement witnesses.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
